steps.prompt: Which are the finite verbs in this sentence?

hints.festinans and manere: 'Festinans' is a participle and 'manere' is an infinitive — neither is finite
hints.descende only: 'Descende' is one finite verb (imperative), but 'oportet' is also finite (indicative)
hints.oportet only: 'Oportet' is finite, but 'descende' is also a finite verb (imperative)

hints.to cut: 'To cut' is 'scindere' — 'descende' comes from 'descendere' (to climb down)

hints.present indicative: Present indicative 2nd sing. would be 'descendis' — 'descende' is imperative
hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive 2nd sing. would be 'descendas' — 'descende' is imperative

hints.ascendo, ascendere, ascendi, ascensum: These are the parts of 'ascendo' (to go up) — the opposite direction
hints.scando, scandere, scandi, scansum: These are the parts of 'scando' (to climb) — 'descendere' is de- + scandere
hints.scindo, scindere, scidi, scissum: These are the parts of 'scindo' (to cut/split)

steps.reveal: festinans = hurrying / in haste (pres. act. participle modifying the subject 'you')

hints.perfect passive participle: Perfect passive participle would end in -tus — 'festinans' has -ans (present participle)
hints.gerundive: Gerundive would be 'festinandus' — 'festinans' is a present participle
hints.finite verb: 'Festinans' is a participle, not a finite verb — it modifies the subject

hints.fero, ferre, tuli, latum: These are the parts of 'fero' (to carry)
hints.finio, finire, finivi, finitum: These are the parts of 'finio' (to finish/limit)
hints.fugio, fugere, fugi, fugitum: These are the parts of 'fugio' (to flee)

steps.reveal: oportet me manere = it is necessary for me to remain

hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'oporteat' — 'oportet' is indicative

hints.operor, operari, operatus sum, —: These are the parts of 'operor' (to work)
hints.opto, optare, optavi, optatum: These are the parts of 'opto' (to wish/choose)
hints.opprimo, opprimere, oppressi, oppressum: These are the parts of 'opprimo' (to crush/overwhelm)

hints.genitive: Genitive would be 'Zacchaei' — 'Zacchae' is vocative of 'Zacchaeus' (1st/2nd decl.)
hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'Zacchaeus' — 'Zacchae' with -ae ending is vocative

steps.reveal: in domo tua = in your house (abl., location)

hints.dative: Dative 'domo' looks the same, but 'in' + ablative = location
hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'domus' — 'domo' is ablative after 'in'

steps.prompt: What case is 'me' and what is its function?
steps.reveal: oportet me manere = it is necessary for me to remain (acc. + inf.)

hints.ablative — after preposition: There is no preposition governing 'me' — it is the accusative subject of 'manere'
hints.dative — indirect object: 'Me' as dative would be 'mihi' — 'me' is accusative here
hints.nominative — subject of 'oportet': 'Oportet' is impersonal — 'me' is accusative, subject of the infinitive

hints.mando, mandare, mandavi, mandatum: These are the parts of 'mando' (to command)
hints.manduco, manducare, manducavi, manducatu: These are the parts of 'manduco' (to eat)
hints.moneo, monere, monui, monitum: These are the parts of 'moneo' (to warn/advise)

translation: Zacchaeus, hurry and come down, for today I must stay at your house
